matches.x: CHAPTER LIT. Christ's ministry and sufferings... wre hath believed. our. report? and to. whom is the arm of the Lord revealed ?

The beginning of this text has a double meaning as spoken by Abinadi: "Who hath believed our report, and to whom is the arm of the Lord revealed?" (Mosiah 14:1). Surely he was delivering a report. This query might be a common cry from every prophet who ever revealed to his people the coming of the Messiah. In this instance it is both Isaiah's and Abinadi's cries that may echo Christ's own lament…

“We expect that he will come again, not the next time as the babe of Bethlehem, not the next time to be despised and rejected of men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief, but as the Lord of life and glory, as the King of Israel to sit upon the throne of his father David, to rule from the rivers to the ends of the earth; not to be brought unto the subjection of men, but to have all things…

The Lord Jesus Christ, the one perfect being in the world, was described by Isaiah as "despised and rejected of men, a man of sorrows; and acquainted with grief." (Isaiah 53:3.) That describes his life, the one perfect life lived in the world, full of love and of service. (George Q. Morris, in Conference Report, Oct. 1958, 72; or Improvement Era, Dec. 1958, 951)

Surely he has borne our, and carried our sorrows: yet we did esteem him stricken, smitten of God, and afflicted. [changes from the Brass plates - see Mosiah 14]
Isaiah 53:4 KJV: Surely he hath borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows
Surely he [has] hath borne our griefs (choliy) literally, "But yet He hath taken (or borne) our sicknesses,"]

“Because He was God-even the Son of God-He could carry the weight and burden of other men's sins on Himself. Isaiah prophesied our Savior's willingness to do this in these words: ‘Surely he has borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows. He was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities: the chastisement of our peace was upon him; and with his stripes we are healed.’ (Isaiah…

wounded for our transgressions/bruised for our iniquities. Wounded (Hebrew chalal) is better translated as "pierced fatally." Bruised (Hebrew daka') is more correctly "crushed." (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.474)

There are at least four uses of the lamb: 1) the Paschal lamb in Exodus 12 which enables the angel of death to pass over the children of Israel 2) Isaiah 53:7 He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth: he is brought as a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so he openeth not his mouth. 3) Lev. 16:21 "And Aaron shall lay both his hands upon…

the Lord shall prosper in his hand.” By and by the Prophet goes on to say, “And who shall declare his generation,” for his life is taken from the earth. If he had no generation, who could declare it? I told you there was an agent who would bring out every subject in bold relief, which is the Holy Ghost, who searcheth all things, even the deep things of God, and until that celestial agent should…

Gods before Jehovah is the first. The second, no graven images. The third, don't take the name of Jehovah in vain. The fourth, is the sign of the covenant; keep the Sabbath day holy. Why would the Sabbath day be the sign of the covenant? How many days did it take the Lord to create the earth? Which day did he rest on? Therefore will I divide him [a portion] with the great, and he shall divide the spoil with the strong; because he hath poured out his soul unto death: and he was numbered with the transgressors; and he bare the sins of many, and made intercession for the transgressors.
